Thank you, Bernardo. So, first of all, I'd like to introduce the case. So, it's patients with, in the past, underwent radiotherapy for lymphoproliferative disease. The left kidney is the hypertrophic kidney, it's a very small kidney.

And on the right side he had developed stenosis some years ago on the level of the pelvic area. and the prepelvic area and so I performed in the past one anastomosis with the recession of the stenosis with anastomosis, terminal-terminal anastomosis but in the future we developed another stenosis at the level of lumbar level and below the OPJU and so at this point considering the quality of the the urethra that is not good quality, the tissue is not perfect because he underwent in the past one operation, he underwent also radiotherapy. I decided on the basis of endoscopic evaluation to replace the urethra with

So, the discussion was this, if it is possible to change, to do a complete substitution of the urethane with the helium, or if it is possible to do only a sub-total substitution because during the operation I needed to evaluate if the quality of the last parts of the ureter, the pelvic ureter was good in terms of quality of tissue or not. On the basis of this I can modulate my operation. Moreover, there is another limitation that the patients have

and implantable prothesis, penile prothesis so we have also the reservoir that limits the access in the area of the bladder and so this was another reason why I needed to evaluate if the substitution of the ureter can be done only for a long tract and not completely At this point, first of all, I put the patients in the flank position and I start

The surgeon changes the patient's position from the flank to a position similar to that used in radical prostatectomy. In this new position, the surgeon dissects the distal part of the ureter and prepares for the resection of the ileum.

The surgeon observes the ureter tightly adhered to the common iliac artery and vein, highlighting the difficulty in dissecting the ureter while ensuring the preservation of these major vessels to prevent complications.

respect a lot and to be very prudent during the decision steps because otherwise the laceration of the vein is a very problem and a very issue during this operation because if you have a laceration alteration an injury of the renal artery, the iliac artery, so you can suture, you can do a suture of the whole of the injury, the area, but I think it is more difficult to treat the injury for the vein.
The surgeon examines the ureter, noting its rigidity and subsequent softening. Using ureteroscopy, the ureter is cut below the iliac vessel, revealing a change in tissue quality from rigid to soft.

So where did you find the right spot to cut exactly? From preoperative stuff or just watching and touching the tissue? Yes, touching first of all, touching and the second obviously with the preoperative assessment with the ureteroscopy, but I think that it is very important to touch the tissue. Look at how the ureter is soft. It is rigid only in the periuretary tissue, but the ureter is soft, so I think the quality of the ureter is good.
The surgeon removes the surrounding fat tissue to clean the area around the urethra, preparing for an anastomosis that involves only the urethral tissue to prevent stenosis.

The surgeon evaluates the distance from the ileocecal valve, marking the proximal part with the first suture approximately 15 to 20 centimeters away. The ileum is assessed to ensure it remains in its physiological position without stretching the tissue.

The surgeon evaluates the length of the ileum in its natural position, measuring twenty centimeters from the iliopsoas wall. A stitch is placed at this point to mark the location, and the distal part of the ileum is suspended using a monlock through the peritoneum.

with the helium positioned in the physiological site so I evaluate the real length of the helium otherwise there is a risk to have not a realistic length and one of the big problems that we have when we do, for example, the neoblader, is to have the small neoblader, not the real, the big neoblader. And so this is the reason why I think it's very important to do this. of the defect of the ureter yes so i i calculated and i i imagine that back on the twenty centimeters because uh usually the ureter is the length of the ureter is twenty five centimeters i imagine that i saved five or six centimeters so this is the reason why i decided to do it So it's what I do usually when I do the urinary derivation and obviously when I do a neobladar. so I isolated and I cutted the helium and so at this point I evaluated the length of the helium the helium is putted in front of the in front of the area of the wire we do, we restore the continuity of the helium, the continuity with the suture.

The surgeon moves forward with the procedure by cutting the corner of the distal part of the ileum that was previously cut. Using a stapler, the continuity of the ileum is restored with a standard technique. The GIEA stapler used has a measurement of 4.5, and the surgeon emphasizes the importance of making two cuts of six centimeters each to ensure sufficient continuity of the ileum.

I use the same instrument and I don't use a special instrument for handling the bowels. This is very important to be very, very delicate. As you know, the whole needle is a little bit traumatic, but as you know, I try to be very delicate. in this way you can save an instrument because with the same instrument I do the radical prostatectomy and the urinary derivation cystectomy and other techniques so I use my standard technique in my standard technique I use only four instruments yes that's a lot of savings yes such approach so

The surgeon suspends the ileum to achieve perfect alignment of its lateral face, ensuring precise suturing. The ileum is rotated to avoid involving unintended structures in the suture. The first and second firings of the stapler are completed to establish continuity of the bowel, preparing for lateral movement of the ileum to perform the anastomosis.

The surgeon is in the Trendelenburg position, continuing the dissection of the mesentery to enhance the mobility of the ileum. The peritoneum is visible, and the ureter is being prepared by eliminating surrounding fat. The ureter appears clean, and the surgeon is ready to perform a lateral terminal anastomosis, with plans to remove the last part of the ileum at the end of the operation.

The surgeon removes the last part of the ileum, ensuring the anastomosis is terminal. Metallic clips are visible in the last section of the ileum, which are removed to prevent stone formation. The procedure concludes with suturing using an ironic suture.
The surgeon performs a single dual running suture on the bowel, opting not to use barbed suture at this stage. Barbed suture will be used later when removing the last parts of the ilium.

I use barbed suture when I do the neobladar, for example. During the configuration of the neobladar I use barbed suture. You have been one of the first laparoscopic and robotic surgeons in Europe and Italy, but you started doing cystectomy pretty recently, isn't it? Yes, I started in the past when I performed a lot of radical cystectomy with the laparoscopic approach, but I think that it was not a safe procedure under the oncology point of view, and now

I switch, when I use, when I perform some radical cystectomy, but I always have some concern about the oncological aspects, and so the indications for me are very, very confined, so it means that usually I use this technique only when the disease is confined to the blood so it means if there are suspect infiltration of the fat or there are big tumors or suspect positive lymph nodes I think that the robotic approach is not a good indication because the risk to have a seeding to have a metastasis so the oncological safety in my opinion is not excellent so I think that you need to apply different principles, oncological principles for urinary tumors, for transitional tumors, for kidney tumors, for prostatic cancer and this is a tumor that we need to respect in terms of oncological point of view, in terms of safety because The risk of recurrence is very high. If you have recurrence, the possibility to save the patients and to treat the patients in a radical way is very, very low. So there is a very big risk of mortality. So this is one procedure. that is very important for patients because they can care for the patients and so this is the reason why we need to select the best procedure for him.

I think this is a very wise observation and I completely agree with you that it's still not clear. And even I think the real advantages of such a reconstructive procedures on the outcome of the patients are probably still not so clear.

But anyway, so that's coming back to the case. So here you decide to do separate stitches rather than a running suture. Let me ask you why you prefer to do a separate rather than a running suture? I think that is so with the running I do have a more risk to have a stenosis I think and this is one of the reason moreover so it's more safe for the stenosis for the anastomosis because if they're running suture so if we have a laceration for example for the tissue when there is not you can have back on there urine leak so instead if you do the single stitch I think that the suture is well done and so also if some stitch obviously have a laceration there is a laceration of the tissue and obviously I think that the risk to have a urine leak is more lower.

The ureteral final stump remains in place, not yet connected to the ileum, creating a visible cul-de-sac. The surgeon plans to remove it after completing the anastomosis to assess the anastomosis length and quality.

The surgeon repositions the patient in the flank position and uses the robotic system to clean the upper parts of the ureter, extending up to the pelvis. The complete ureter is removed in preparation for the anastomosis, with the choice of the safe part of the ureter based on tissue elasticity.

The surgeon removes surrounding fat and tests the elasticity of the ureter, assessing its condition for further surgical intervention. The ureter's quality is compromised due to previous radiotherapy, affecting its vascularization and increasing the risk of stenosis.

stenosis after a iatrogenic damage on the ureteroscopy and other reasons because you have to consider that in this case the ureter is the tissue condition is not good and the quality of the vascularization is not good in the whole length of the ureter not in a segmental part so the disease is not confined to the ureter, especially in the segmental area, but the disease of the ureter involves completely the ureter in all the lengths of the ureter itself.

The surgeon identifies the pelvis and removes surrounding fat to ensure a tension-free environment for the anastomosis. The tissue quality appears good, but there is slight tension observed. To alleviate this, the surgeon dissects and mobilizes the kidney downward to facilitate a tension-free anastomosis.

I understand that the tension was more related to the meso rather than the length of the... Yes, yes, yes, it's true but as you know when you use the staplers for and with this technique and in many and also whenever you do with the neoblader or when you do with the the Bricker for example, the Meso is short and usually it is not possible to have a good, perfect length of Meso, it is not soft and so this is the reason why sometimes it is important to prolong the diatization of the Meso in order to have a good length of the meso to avoid the tension. But sometimes if you do this dissection of the meso with bipolar etc. you need some time to give some stitch and so there is a risk to involve big vessels and so perhaps there is a little bit of risk for vascularization of the last part of the ilium.
The surgeon removes fat surrounding the kidney and approximates the kidney to the ilium, revealing softer tissue compared to previous sections.
